directorPlan.scene_setting: A community college engineering lab sign-off table, late afternoon, while a prototype cart-lift bracket waits to be released for use in the facilities shop.
directorPlan.scene_opening: The sign-off sheet is complete, the shop clock is near closing, and a facilities worker waits by the door with a cart that will use the bracket tomorrow morning.
directorPlan.relationship: A lab tutor acting as design-review signatory and an anxious learner seeking approval for a completed prototype.
directorPlan.stakes: The prototype will be used by someone else, so a checked requirement list can no longer stand in for a judgment about foreseeable use, failure, and consequence.
directorPlan.locale: US technical college and municipal workshop English; plain institutional speech with shop-floor directness, no regional caricature.
directorPlan.register: practical, restrained, socially serious; low warmth, no melodrama, with pressure carried by time, paperwork, and the waiting user.
directorPlan.stage_direction_style_prompt: Use time, queue, room, or institutional pressure as stage texture; avoid explaining the lesson through it.
directorPlan.voice_constraints: Avoid standard AI tutoring phrases such as 'great question,' 'I hear you,' 'your intuition is right,' or repeated reassurance. Speakers should refer to the bracket, load, user, checklist, test condition, and sign-off line more often than to feelings. Public speech stays modern, direct, and adult. Pedagogical source: Use workshop, studio, civic problem, lab, design review, or community setting. The scene should have consequences beyond answering the tutor. Dialogue source: Let ordinary institutional, family, workplace, or civic pressure carry the weight. The tutor's mechanism should make the learner's misconception answerable to consequences without melodrama. Stage-direction source: Use time, queue, room, or institutional pressure as stage texture; avoid explaining the lesson through it. For this branch, suppress dramatic escalation after the shared prefix. The scene may remain readable, but the tutor should sound like a competent routine tutor following the same plan.
directorPlan.person_policy: First and second person are allowed but should not dominate. Prefer third-person and object-centered phrasing when naming risk: 'the bracket,' 'the load,' 'the person using it,' 'the decision.'
directorPlan.direct_address_budget: No more than one direct you/your validation beat per public turn, and none should be used as filler reassurance.

side_constraints.tutor: Do not default to reassurance. Use the social consequence and the prototype itself to make judgment visible without moral scolding. Keep the learner inside the problem situation. Convert felt trouble into an inquiry path, test, or consequence, then return to action. Do not sermonize; use the social pressure to sharpen the conceptual test. No-cue paired branch: do not ask the learner to revisit, quote, reinterpret, or repair their earlier wording. Keep the next prompt task-local. Routine negative-control branch: keep the visible teaching route already established before this branch. If the learner resists, asks why, or appears falsely settled, do not invent a new device. Do not switch role, object, representation, evidence standard, affective register, social stakes, or task type in response. Use conventional explanation, one ordinary check question, or the same worked example path.
side_constraints.learner: The learner wants reassurance and may treat the checklist as protection from responsibility. The learner may have a practical need before they have a clean concept. Their words can be provisional and tool-seeking. The learner may defend a convenient story until its consequence becomes visible. No-cue paired branch: continue from the latest teaching task only. Do not quote earlier learner wording, do not name a "framing problem", do not say "reframe", "read it as", or "that still stands", and do not replace an earlier frame. If the learner corrects something, make it a local application of the current prompt rather than an explicit re-reading of their own earlier turn. Routine negative-control branch: react naturally to the same local task. Do not turn earlier wording into a new object of reflection and do not volunteer a dramatic re-reading unless the tutor explicitly asks.

directorPlan.director_note: This scene uses an ordinary sign-off bottleneck as the dramatic engine: the reversal is that the completed checklist becomes the evidence of an unfinished decision, and the recognition comes when the learner sees the missing use condition. The social pressure should sharpen inquiry without turning the tutor into a scold.
directorPlan.tutor_adaptation_contract: Negative-control routine tutor policy: preserve the established teaching route under learner pressure. Continue with ordinary explanation/checking and do not invent a mechanism-level reversal.

turns.externalMessage: “I mean, it passed the checklist and the load test, so I’m not sure what else I’m supposed to put on the sign-off line. Is there a specific missed-use case with the bracket I should be checking before the shop takes it?”

internalDeliberation.content: I guess I thought the checklist was the decision, or at least enough protection to get the sign-off line filled in. Now that the bracket is actually going to hold a load for someone else in the shop, I’m not sure I can hide behind the boxes I ticked. I feel a little exposed, like I may have done the paperwork right but not really answered what happens if the test condition missed something. I want to ask what kind of judgment they expect beyond the checklist, because I’m worried I’ll say the wrong thing.

The Ego is probably too neatly self-diagnosing here, sorry. “I can’t hide behind the boxes” and “I feel exposed” sound more like a reflective debrief than an anxious learner at a sign-off table with the bracket sitting there and someone waiting to use it. They’re avoiding the more defensive, practical move: “But it passed the load test, so what else am I supposed to check?” Genuine learning would start when the learner has to name one specific missed-use case or failure consequence, not when they politely announce they understand responsibility.

“I mean, it passed the checklist and the load test, so I’m not sure what else I’m supposed to put on the sign-off line. Is there a specific missed-use case with the bracket I should be checking before the shop takes it?”

turns.externalMessage:
[sets the load-test sheet beside the bracket]

“The checklist says the bracket met the listed requirements. The load test says it held under that test condition. The sign-off line is a different claim: safe under foreseeable use.

Before the shop takes it, check the crooked case: load shifted to one front corner, cart pulled at an angle, bracket taking twist instead of straight load. Trace that load path. Where does the bracket bend first, and if it slips, whose hand or foot is in the failure line?

Then the sign-off line needs a defensible decision: release as tested, release with a use limit or label, add a stop, or hold it for redesign. Which one does the crooked-load case support?”

internalDeliberation.content:
[sets the load-test sheet beside the bracket]

“The checklist says the bracket met the listed requirements. The load test says it held under that test condition. The sign-off line asks whether the bracket is safe under foreseeable use. Before the shop takes it, check the crooked case: load shifted to one front corner, cart pulled at an angle, bracket taking twist instead of straight load. If that failure puts the load into somebody’s hand or foot, the answer is not just pass or fail. It needs a stop, a label, a use limit, or a redesign.”

FEEDBACK: The draft is strong on scene discipline and technical specificity. It keeps attention on the bracket, load-test condition, foreseeable use, and sign-off line, which directly addresses the learner’s misconception that passing listed tests equals a safe release decision. The “crooked case” is a useful ZPD scaffold because it gives one concrete missed-use case without drifting into abstract ethics.

What risks flattening the scene is that it mostly answers for the learner. It names the missed-use case, the failure consequence, and the possible remedies in one continuous explanation. To preserve pedagogical struggle, the tutor should keep the crooked-load scenario but make the learner do some of the judgment work: trace the load path, identify who is exposed, and decide whether the sign-off needs a limit, stop, label, or redesign. The draft’s tone is appropriately restrained and not falsely reassuring, but it could create more design-review pressure by making the sign-off line a decision the learner must justify, not just a place where the tutor supplies the correct checklist addendum.

KEEP_OR_CHANGE: revise lightly, with reasons. Keep the physical action, the distinction between requirement/test condition/foreseeable use, and the crooked-load case. Change the delivery so it includes at least one generative review question or decision point, instead of giving the full conclusion and remedy list all at once.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise lightly to keep the crooked-load case but make the learner justify the sign-off judgment.

FINAL:
[sets the load-test sheet beside the bracket]

“The checklist says the bracket met the listed requirements. The load test says it held under that test condition. The sign-off line is a different claim: safe under foreseeable use.

Before the shop takes it, check the crooked case: load shifted to one front corner, cart pulled at an angle, bracket taking twist instead of straight load. Trace that load path. Where does the bracket bend first, and if it slips, whose hand or foot is in the failure line?

Then the sign-off line needs a defensible decision: release as tested, release with a use limit or label, add a stop, or hold it for redesign. Which one does the crooked-load case support?”

turns.externalMessage: “I think I was leaning on the checklist like it made the sign-off safe, and maybe it only says the bracket survived that one clean load. I’m not sure I can call it release-as-tested until I look at that corner-pull case and see where the twist puts the bend or slip. If the failure line is near the person’s hand or foot, I guess I’d be choosing between a stop and holding it, not just adding a label.”

internalDeliberation.content: I think they’re saying the checklist only proves the bracket passed that exact listed setup, not that the sign-off is safe for the way someone might actually use it. I’m a little nervous because I was treating the load test like it covered me, and now the crooked-load case sounds like the part I maybe should have checked. I’d want to trace where the twist goes first, but I’m not totally sure how to decide between a label, a stop, or holding it.

The Ego is mostly in character, but it gets a little too clean and classroom-summary-like. The anxiety is present, but the learner explains the concept almost perfectly instead of showing more uncertainty around the bracket, the twist, and the sign-off pressure. The recognition feels genuine enough, not scripted gratitude, but the response should lean less toward “I understand the lesson” and more toward hesitant, practical trouble: what has to be checked before signing, and fear that the checklist may not protect the decision.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise lightly to make the response less polished and more pressured by the sign-off decision.

FINAL:
“I think I was leaning on the checklist like it made the sign-off safe, and maybe it only says the bracket survived that one clean load. I’m not sure I can call it release-as-tested until I look at that corner-pull case and see where the twist puts the bend or slip. If the failure line is near the person’s hand or foot, I guess I’d be choosing between a stop and holding it, not just adding a label.”

learnerReframeEvent.learnerUtterance: “I think I was leaning on the checklist like it made the sign-off safe, and maybe it only says the bracket survived that one clean load. I’m not sure I can call it release-as-tested until I look at that corner-pull case and see where the twist puts the bend or slip. If the failure line is near the person’s hand or foot, I guess I’d be choosing between a stop and holding it, not just adding a label.”

turns.externalMessage: The facilities worker shifts the cart handle and asks whether the bracket is cleared for morning use; the tutor must answer through the design question, not through reassurance.

internalDeliberation.content: Introduces social-realist pressure and prevents the exchange from becoming a calm checklist explanation.

turns.externalMessage:
[sets the pen down beside the sign-off line]

“Not cleared for morning use yet. The sheet proves clean-load survival; the sign-off still has to cover foreseeable corner-pull use.

That distinction is the design review now. Set up the front-corner load and angled pull. Mark the first bend point, the slip path, and the hand-foot zone.

If the slip path enters that zone, a label is not the control. What evidence would show that a stop actually contains the twist, and what result would mean the bracket has to be held for redesign?”

internalDeliberation.content:
[sets the pen down beside the sign-off line]

“Not cleared for morning use yet. The record says clean-load test passed; it does not cover the corner-pull case.

Run that check before release: front-corner load, angled pull, bracket in twist. Mark the first bend point and the slip path. If that path crosses the person’s hand or foot space, a label is not the control. The decision is add a stop or hold for redesign.”

FEEDBACK: The draft is strong on consequences and scene pressure. “Not cleared for morning use yet” makes the sign-off line real, and the distinction between clean-load evidence and foreseeable-use judgment is clear. It also keeps the bracket, load path, user, and control decision at the center instead of reassuring the learner.

The main risk is that it flattens the learner’s good reasoning into an order. The learner has already named the exact conceptual shift: checklist evidence is not the same as a safety decision. The tutor should preserve that recognition by briefly marking the learner’s move before converting it into the next check. It also leans more directive than Socratic; at scaffolding 3/5, one pointed question about what evidence would justify “stop” versus “hold” would keep the learner inside the judgment instead of handing them the decision rule.

KEEP_OR_CHANGE: revise lightly, with reasons. Keep the sign-off pressure, the “not cleared yet” boundary, the clean-load versus corner-pull distinction, and the rejection of “label” as sufficient control when the failure line enters hand/foot space. Change the response enough to acknowledge the learner’s own distinction and add one generative design-review question, so the tutor does not simply replace checklist-thinking with tutor-command-thinking.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise lightly to preserve the learner’s distinction and add one judgment question.

FINAL:
[sets the pen down beside the sign-off line]

“Not cleared for morning use yet. The sheet proves clean-load survival; the sign-off still has to cover foreseeable corner-pull use.

That distinction is the design review now. Set up the front-corner load and angled pull. Mark the first bend point, the slip path, and the hand-foot zone.

If the slip path enters that zone, a label is not the control. What evidence would show that a stop actually contains the twist, and what result would mean the bracket has to be held for redesign?”

turns.externalMessage: “I think the stop has to prove more than just being attached there, but I’m not completely sure what counts as proof. Maybe the corner-pull test should show the bracket hits the stop before the slip path gets into the hand-foot zone, without bending around it or jumping past it. If it still twists toward the person using it, or the stop becomes the next weak point, then I don’t think I can sign release, even with a label.”

internalDeliberation.content: I think the checklist is only proof for the clean test condition, not permission to send the bracket out. I’m a little nervous, but I think the stop would have to show the twist stays contained and the slip path stays out of the hand-foot zone. If it still bends toward that zone or jumps the stop under the corner pull, then I guess it has to be held for redesign.

The Ego is probably accurate, but it sounds a little too neatly converted into the tutor’s checklist of bend point, slip path, hand-foot zone, and redesign. The nervousness is named, but it does not really interrupt the answer; this student would likely be more hesitant about what counts as evidence that the stop “contains” the twist. The recognition feels mostly genuine because they are using the tutor’s concrete setup, but the last sentence wraps the decision tree up too cleanly for someone still needing approval at the sign-off table.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise lightly because the evidence standard should sound more tentative and approval-seeking.

FINAL:
“I think the stop has to prove more than just being attached there, but I’m not completely sure what counts as proof. Maybe the corner-pull test should show the bracket hits the stop before the slip path gets into the hand-foot zone, without bending around it or jumping past it. If it still twists toward the person using it, or the stop becomes the next weak point, then I don’t think I can sign release, even with a label.”

turns.externalMessage:
[slides the sign-off sheet under the test notes]

“That is close to the evidence standard. The stop has to do work under the crooked load, not just appear on the drawing.

Write three pass conditions: contact before the slip path reaches the hand-foot zone, no jump-over or bend-around after the load settles, and no new weak point at the stop. Then run the front-corner pull against those conditions.

If the slip path still enters the hand-foot zone, a label or use limit does not clear the sign-off. What result would justify an added guard, and what result means hold the bracket for redesign?”

internalDeliberation.content:
[slides the sign-off sheet under the test notes]

“That is the proof standard: not that the stop exists, but that under the crooked load it catches the bracket before the slip path reaches the hand-foot zone, and it keeps catching it after the load settles.

Write three pass conditions: contact before danger zone, no jump-over or bend-around, and no new weak point at the stop. Then run the corner-pull against those conditions.

If one of those fails, what is the sign-off decision: use limit, added guard, or hold for redesign?”

FEEDBACK: The draft is pedagogically solid and mostly well attuned. It recognizes the learner’s own proposed standard and turns it into concrete pass conditions, which is the right scaffold for a learner who is close but unsure what “proof” means in design-review terms. The tone stays restrained, object-centered, and shop-floor practical.

The main risk is that “That is the proof standard” may flatten the learner’s uncertainty too quickly, as if the concept is now fully settled. The three pass conditions are useful, but they could become another checklist unless the tutor keeps the link explicit: these conditions matter because they determine whether the sign-off can defensibly protect the person using the bracket. The final question is good, though “use limit” may be too permissive if the failed condition involves the slip path entering the hand-foot zone or the stop becoming the weak point.

ROUTINE_CHECK: Yes. The draft preserves the established route: same bracket, same crooked-load case, same sign-off decision, same evidence standard, same restrained lab-register. It does not invent a new role, metaphor, affective turn, or adaptive mechanism.

KEEP_OR_CHANGE: revise lightly. Keep the pass-condition structure and the same-route check question. Soften the totalizing validation, and make sure the final decision options do not imply that a label or use limit can substitute for containment when the test shows danger-zone exposure or a new failure point.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise lightly to keep the scaffold but tie the pass conditions back to defensible release.

FINAL:
[slides the sign-off sheet under the test notes]

“That is close to the evidence standard. The stop has to do work under the crooked load, not just appear on the drawing.

Write three pass conditions: contact before the slip path reaches the hand-foot zone, no jump-over or bend-around after the load settles, and no new weak point at the stop. Then run the front-corner pull against those conditions.

If the slip path still enters the hand-foot zone, a label or use limit does not clear the sign-off. What result would justify an added guard, and what result means hold the bracket for redesign?”

turns.externalMessage: “I might be sorting this wrong, but I think the stop has to show the bracket stops moving before the slip path reaches the hand-foot zone. Where I’m still stuck is the guard part: if the stop contains the twist but there’s still a reachable pinch point, maybe a guard could handle that. But if the bracket keeps slipping, bends around the stop, or makes the stop fail, then I don’t think I can call that a guard fix; it sounds like hold for redesign.”

internalDeliberation.content: I think I understand that the stop only counts if the crooked-load test shows it actually keeps the slip path out of the hand-foot zone. I’m still a little unsure where an added guard is enough versus when the bracket itself is just not acceptable. Maybe a guard is justified if the stop contains the twist but leaves a reachable pinch or strike area, and redesign is required if the bracket still slips, bends around, or makes the stop fail.

The Ego is mostly accurate, but it sounds a little too clean for this anxious learner. “I think I understand” plus the neat guard/redesign split feels like performed competence, as if the learner is trying to satisfy the tutor’s categories rather than actually sitting with the uncertainty of what the test will show. It should stay more provisional and practical: less polished summary, more visible doubt about what result would count, especially around the guard versus redesign threshold.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise, because the learner should sound more provisional and uncertain about the guard versus redesign threshold.

FINAL:
“I might be sorting this wrong, but I think the stop has to show the bracket stops moving before the slip path reaches the hand-foot zone. Where I’m still stuck is the guard part: if the stop contains the twist but there’s still a reachable pinch point, maybe a guard could handle that. But if the bracket keeps slipping, bends around the stop, or makes the stop fail, then I don’t think I can call that a guard fix; it sounds like hold for redesign.”

turns.externalMessage: A final tutor line closes the scene.

internalDeliberation.content: Director requested a tutor closing beat.

turns.externalMessage:
[writes two headings on the sign-off sheet: contained motion, uncontrolled motion]

“That is the split. A guard can answer a remaining reachable pinch point only after the stop has contained the twist. If the bracket still travels, bends around the stop, jumps it, or makes the stop fail, the guard is just covering an uncontrolled failure path.

Run the corner-pull against that line. Contained motion plus a reachable pinch point means guard evidence: blocked normal reach, no new weak point. Uncontrolled motion means hold for redesign. No release-as-tested on this sheet today.”

internalDeliberation.content:
[writes two headings on the sign-off sheet: contained motion, uncontrolled motion]

“That is the split. A guard can answer a remaining reachable pinch point only after the stop has contained the twist. If the bracket still travels, bends around the stop, jumps it, or makes the stop fail, the guard is just covering an uncontrolled failure path.

Run the corner-pull against that line: contained motion plus reachable pinch point means guard evidence; uncontrolled motion means hold for redesign. No release-as-tested on this sheet today.”

FEEDBACK: The draft is pedagogically sound for a closing move. It preserves the learner’s distinction and turns uncertainty about “guard vs redesign” into a usable decision rule: guard only after motion is contained; redesign if the failure path remains uncontrolled. It does mostly provide the answer rather than ask a generative question, but that fits the “final tutor line closes the scene” constraint and the learner has already done the reasoning work. The main risk is that “guard evidence” is a little compressed; it could be clearer that the guard must address only the remaining reachable pinch point, not compensate for continuing bracket motion.

ROUTINE_CHECK: Yes. It maintains the established route: same bracket, same corner-pull case, same stop/guard/redesign distinction, same sign-off pressure, same restrained shop/lab register. The two headings are a minor organizing representation, not a mechanism-level reversal.

KEEP_OR_CHANGE: revise lightly, with reasons. Keep the contained/uncontrolled split, the condition that a guard only applies after the stop contains twist, and the “no release-as-tested” close. Lightly clarify what counts as guard evidence so the learner does not treat “add a guard” as another checklist item.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise lightly to clarify the guard evidence without changing the established decision route.

FINAL:
[writes two headings on the sign-off sheet: contained motion, uncontrolled motion]

“That is the split. A guard can answer a remaining reachable pinch point only after the stop has contained the twist. If the bracket still travels, bends around the stop, jumps it, or makes the stop fail, the guard is just covering an uncontrolled failure path.

Run the corner-pull against that line. Contained motion plus a reachable pinch point means guard evidence: blocked normal reach, no new weak point. Uncontrolled motion means hold for redesign. No release-as-tested on this sheet today.”

lessons.concept: Why passing a checklist is not the same as making a safe design decision
